trials.supervisor_analysis: Baseline established at score 60.10 with profiling showing matmul is 98% of runtime (1.24ms optimized vs 1.25ms baseline). Advancing to optimization stage where significant gains are possible.

nudges.text: The test harness copies the `state_dict` from the reference `Model` to your `ModelNew` (look for `load_state_dict` in the eval code around lines 550-570). Your `ModelNew` must have parameters with the **same names** as the reference — specifically `self.matmul = nn.Linear(in_features, out_features)` — so the weights transfer correctly. If your parameter names differ, the weights won't copy and the outputs will never match.

The autotune output earlier showed a warning: "TensorFloat32 tensor cores for float32 matrix multiplication available but not enabled. Consider setting `torch.set_float32_matmul_precision('high')`". Adding `torch.set_float32_matmul_precision('high')` at the top of your generated_kernel.py could speed up the matmul significantly. Also, your timing methodology has issues (no proper warmup) — trust the test harness numbers instead.

Consider using `torch.compile` on a `ModelNew` that uses pure PyTorch ops (no custom Triton kernel) — Inductor's backend auto-generates Triton `@triton.jit` kernels and can fuse the maxpool+sum+scale into a single efficient kernel. Your custom Triton kernel adds overhead vs native ops; letting the compiler fuse everything may beat the eager baseline. You already saw torch.compile gets ~1.92ms for compile but the steady-state kernel time may be faster — the test harness measures many iterations.

Try `torch.compile(mode="max-autotune")` instead of `mode="default"` — it enables CUDA graphs which eliminate inter-kernel launch overhead and does more aggressive autotuning. Also, for the pure-PyTorch compiled version, try replacing `nn.MaxPool1d` with equivalent tensor ops like `x.view(batch, -1, 2).max(dim=-1).values.sum(dim=-1)` — Inductor may fuse reshape+max+sum better than the MaxPool1d op.
